---
title: Signing keys
description: How tokens are signed, how the JWKS document is published, and how to control key material.
---

# Signing keys

Vefa.CustomAuth signs JWT access and ID tokens with an RSA key. The **public** half is published
as a JSON Web Key Set (JWKS); the **private** half never leaves the server. Resource servers and
clients fetch the JWKS to validate signatures.

## Two key sources

### 1. Store-backed auto-generated key (default)

`AddJwtTokenSigning()` registers an RSA signing provider that **auto-generates** a key on first
use and **persists** it through `ICustomAuthSigningKeyStore`. With a durable store (EF Core /
MongoDB) the key survives restarts and is shared by every instance that reads the same store.

```csharp
builder.Services
    .AddCustomAuth(options => { /* … */ })
    .AddJwtTokenSigning();   // store-backed RSA key
```

This is the simplest option and works well when all instances share a persistent store.

> **Warning — in-memory storage.** With the in-memory signing key store, a freshly generated key
> exists only for the lifetime of the process and is **not shared** across instances. Every
> restart invalidates previously issued tokens, and multiple instances sign with different keys.
> Use a durable store or a fixed certificate outside of single-process development.

### 2. Fixed X.509 signing certificate

For operator-controlled key material that is identical across a load-balanced farm, supply a
certificate. This **replaces** the store-backed provider:

```csharp
// From an X509Certificate2 you load yourself:
customAuthBuilder.AddSigningCertificate(certificate);                 // RS256 by default
customAuthBuilder.AddSigningCertificate(certificate, SecurityAlgorithms.RsaSha384);

// Or from a PFX/PKCS#12 file:
customAuthBuilder.AddSigningCertificate("Keys/signing.pfx", pfxPassword);
```

The certificate must contain an **RSA private key**. When configured, the certificate wins over
the store-backed provider regardless of registration order.

## The JWKS endpoint

`MapCustomAuthEndpoints()` publishes the public keys at `/.well-known/jwks.json`. The discovery
document advertises this via `jwks_uri`. A typical resource-server setup points JWT bearer
validation at the issuer, and ASP.NET Core fetches and caches the JWKS automatically:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddAuthentication(JwtBearerDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)
    .AddJwtBearer(options =>
    {
        options.Authority = "https://auth.example.com"; // discovers jwks_uri
        // …
    });
```

The published JWK exposes only `kty`, `use`, `alg`, `kid`, `n`, `e` — public RSA parameters. No
private material is ever included.

## Algorithm support

- The store-backed provider signs with **RS256**, and the discovery document advertises
  `id_token_signing_alg_values_supported = ["RS256"]`.
- The certificate path accepts an explicit algorithm argument (default `RS256`).

## Key rotation

Because validators fetch keys by `kid` from the JWKS, rotation is a matter of introducing a new
key and retiring the old one once outstanding tokens have expired. See
[Rotating signing keys](/docs/recipes#rotating-signing-keys).

## Protecting private key material at rest

When using the store-backed key, the private material lives in your database. Protect it with the
same controls you apply to other secrets:

- Encrypt the database / collection at rest.
- Restrict database access.
- Prefer a fixed certificate sourced from a secret manager or key vault for production.

<!-- TODO: Document a built-in encryption/extension point for signing key material at rest if/when
the library adds one. As of v1.0, encryption of the store-backed private key is the operator's
